event: Madonna and Child with the Infant Saint John and an Angel
symbols: fruit held by the angel — the fruit of redemption, recalling the fruit of the Tree of Knowledge reversed by the fruit of Mary's womb
nude Christ Child — the full humanity of the Incarnate Word, divine vulnerability in human flesh
Virgin Mary's tender gaze — maternal love that is also the Church's contemplation of the mystery of the Incarnation
infant John the Baptist — the forerunner who even from infancy recognizes and defers to Christ (Luke 1:41-44)
angel attending the Holy Family — heaven's participation in and adoration of the mystery of the Incarnation
flowing drapery — the grace and beauty surrounding the sacred figures, the garment of salvation
description: A richly painted Baroque composition showing the Virgin Mary with flowing hair and luminous skin, gazing tenderly downward at the Christ Child who reclines across her lap. The nude infant Christ is animated and playful, reaching outward. Below, a young child — the infant Saint John — looks upward toward Christ. Above and behind the Virgin, an angel with dark wings holds a round fruit, leaning in toward the group. The figures are arranged in a closely interlocking pyramidal composition.
theological_description: Procaccini's intimate devotional painting presents the Madonna and Child with the infant Baptist and an attending angel, a subject deeply rooted in the theology of the Incarnation. The Christ Child's full nudity emphasizes the central Christological doctrine that the eternal Word truly became flesh (John 1:14) — not in appearance but in the complete vulnerability of human infancy. Mary, as Theotokos (God-bearer), is the locus of this mystery: through her consent at the Annunciation, divinity entered creation through the most humble human means. The presence of the infant John the Baptist recalls the Visitation narrative (Luke 1:39-45), when the unborn John leaped in Elizabeth's womb at the approach of the pregnant Mary — the forerunner recognizing his Lord even before birth. John's upward gaze toward Christ prefigures his adult testimony: 'Behold, the Lamb of God' (John 1:29). The angel offering fruit introduces a rich symbolic layer: it may allude to the reversal of the Fall, where the fruit of the forbidden tree brought death, but the fruit of Mary's womb brings eternal life. The Church Fathers consistently taught that Mary is the New Eve whose obedience undoes the disobedience of the first Eve (Irenaeus, Against Heresies III.22.4). The intimate tenderness of the scene invites the viewer into a devotional contemplation of the mystery that the Almighty God chose to enter the world as a helpless infant, held in a mother's arms.

scripture_refs.reason: The Word became flesh and dwelt among us — the theological foundation of every Madonna and Child image
The Visitation: the infant John leaps in Elizabeth's womb at the approach of Mary bearing Christ — the source for the infant Baptist's presence
She gave birth to her firstborn son — the Nativity event underlying all Madonna and Child devotional imagery
Behold the Lamb of God — the infant John's gaze toward Christ prefigures his adult prophetic testimony
When the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son, born of woman — the Pauline theology of the Incarnation through Mary
Blessed is the fruit of your womb — the angel's fruit offering may symbolize the blessed fruit of Mary's motherhood
The virgin shall conceive and bear a son — the Isaianic prophecy fulfilled in the Incarnation scene depicted

_meta.title: Maria Mit Dem Kind, Johannesknaben Und Einem Engel
